-- 1 of 15 --

Erickson’s Work Spread
Through His Talented Students
Below are listed some of the Schools of thought and therapy
which came from studying Milton Erickson’s work
Margaret Mead studied with Erickson for 40 years. Later her
work with Gregory Bateson also a student of Erickson and
Lawerence Kubie, psychoanalyst, led to the formation of the
field of cybernetics.
Jay Haley studied with Erickson for 17 years and developed
Strategic Family Therapy.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 15 --

Writings and workshops by Richard Bandler and John Grinder who co-created of Neuro-
linguistic programming (NLP), a methodology intended to understand and change human
behavior-patterns. He also developed other systems named Design Human Engineering
(DHE) and Neuro Hypnotic Repatterning (NHR).
At one point Gregory Bateson had suggested to Bandler and Grinder that they study with
Milton Erickson M.D., the famous Hypnotherapist in Phoenix, Arizona. Milton Erickson
M.D. opened up a whole new world for them and greatly influenced NLP from then on.
Bandler and Grinder used their unique approach to model Erickson by immersing
themselves into his world and become masters of hypnosis themselves. 	They published
"Patterns of the Hypnotic Techniques of Milton H. Erickson. M.D." in 1975 and a second
volume in collaboration with Judith DeLozier in 1977. This is a major contribution to the
field of hypnosis. Milton Erickson M.D. himself wrote:
"Patterns of the Hypnotic Techniques of Milton H. Erickson, M.D. by Richard Bandler and John
Grinder is a delightful simplification of the infinite complexities of the language I use with patients.
In reading this book, I learned a great deal about the things that I've done without knowing about
them." 	- 	Milton Erickson
Above Quotes from The Solutions Group
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 15 --

Ernest Rossi in Hypnotic Realities and Hypnotherapy, which he co-wrote with
Erickson himself, has broken down Erickson’s Hypnotic inductions and indirect forms
of suggestion into five stages: 1. fixation of attention 2. depotentiating habitual
frameworks and belief systems 3. unconscious search 4. unconscious process 5.
hypnotic response
Erickson and Rossi have called this “Utilization Approach to Hypnotherapy”
Jeffrey Zeig has written A Teaching Seminar With Milton H. Erickson. In this book
he covers the uses of anecdotes in therapy. Jeffrey Zeig, Phd, is the Founder and CEO
of The Milton H. Erickson Foundation. and architect of The Evolution of
Psychotherapy Conference, the Brief Therapy Conference, the Couples Conference,
and the International Congresses on Ericksonian Approaches to Psychotherapy.
Cloe, Mark and Myself are continuing the tradition of honoring and learning from
Milton H. Erickson through our work in Strategic Intervention Coaching and training
you. This lecture is my way of sharing what I appreciate most about this fabulous
book focussed on Erickson’s use of stories.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 15 --

Trance, according to Erickson, is the state in which learning and openness to
change are most likely to occur.
How do you use trance in your own life?
Have you ever tried to relax into the other part of your brain, memory and heart?
Have you tried this before speaking to a client or giving a presentation?
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 15 --

Unconscious Learning
• 	When we are in a trance, even a light one, we more easily accept a suggestion.
• 	When a suggestion doesn’t match a client’s values they won’t accept it. This is GREAT
news, right?
• 	Suggestions may be indirect- A story you share about another person.
• 	Suggestions can be interspersed in an ordinary conversation.
• 	Part of an interesting tale. Feel free to use these same tales by Erickson in your work.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 15 --

Some ways to use stories in coaching:
Tell your client a personal story from your life or work with another person.
Remember whenever you talk about another person your client is thinking about
themselves.
Use folktales and myths about a quest. Your listener will naturally relate themselves
into the role of the character on the quest or journey.
Intros for telling a story to a client:
You are reminding me of this story.
When I talk to you I think of a sweet client I once had.
Your challenge makes me think of a story told by one of my favorite teachers, Miltion
Erickson… (or someone else)
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 15 --

Hypnosis
Defined by Erickson as, “The evocation and utilization of
unconscious learning.”
The client is able to get in touch with their own self
knowledge without the daily mind getting in the way.
The heart of Erickson’s suggestions are to get the client in
touch with their growth and “opening.” By doing this
changes are self reinforcing and lead to further growth and
self understanding.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 15 --

Erickson emphasizes the
importance of an individual’s
uniqueness.
Remember this when working with
the six human needs. NO ONE’s
needs are the same. Everyone has
their own rule book, priorities, and
personal expression, past, and
relationships. Use your love of the
individual qualities in each client to
guide your questions, stories, and
work with that client.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 15 --

Positive Attitude and Input Creates Change
By doing nothing and simultaneously everything – Through your positive attitudes
and beliefs you help your clients.
When you are working with a client, your attitude is that you believe in their ability
to make small changes. You believe in a general way in the possibility of growth and
happiness. Your attitude creates the foundation and the steps of change.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 15 --

Knowing human nature. The need and forces in a person to be
competitive, to desire love, to give back, are all ways in which you, like Erickson,
can help your clients.
When you know your client, you know what will work. When you know a general
need in most parents, for example, is to feel appreciated by their children, you
speak to the way your suggested action will increase their child’s appreciation of
them in the long run. Especially when asking a person to apologize or do
something emotionally difficult, find a meaning which allows them to feel good
about themselves.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 15 --

How Erickson and you can use metaphors to help your client create change.
Erickson not only points out a metaphor, but he works within the metaphor to
bring about a change. Think of the woman who is ashamed of farting. This is a
physical metaphor that when overcome can change the life of the person.
Erickson speaks to her about the wonders of the human body and how it can
keep in and let out different things: air, liquid, solid matter. The woman’s
feelings about her body and it’s natural functions could have been turned into a
whole other type of therapy. However, Erickson, stays within the physical
metaphor itself. His instruction to her to eat beans and learn to fart naturally and
in all different ways creates not only a resolution with the feelings of shame at
farting, but also in the future she will become more comfortable with herself. He
shares in his tale how he visits her and she nurses her baby openly. What we
assume as a listener is not only did she become comfortable with her body, she
found love, and started a family. As a listener, a person is always looking for how
this tale relates to them, what will they gain. They may not need to learn to fart,
but they may want to find a partner or start a family.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 15 --

Small Changes are always possible
Repeatedly Erickson teaches us that when a client makes a small
change, it is the beginning of a bigger and ultimately
transformative life change. Remember the girl who learns to stop
herself from bedwetting? This starts with the realization that, if a
man opened the door on her in the bathroom, she would stop
urinating. Remember the man who believed he had no stomach?
When Erickson feeds him things which make him burp the man
must begin to change. One doesn’t burp with out a stomach.
Think of the nail biter that Erickson asks to only bite one nail.
The young man realizes that he is capable of not biting 10 nails
and instead choosing only one. This changes how he sees his
terrible habit.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 15 --

Desiring Change
Sometimes a persons problem is they don’t
truly want to change. They have their
reward and punishment system confused.
Erickson at times increases a problem until
the client wants to decrease it. Think of
the woman who would lose weight and
then celebrate by gaining weight. Erickson
asked her to gain weight and then she
celebrated by losing weight. This is not a
suggested way for you to work as a health
coach but it is an important example of
how we use rewards unconsciously.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 15 --

S
This happens often with couples. One person asks for love
through picking a fight. They’ve replaced attention-seeking
actions for love making actions. Similar to this, is the person
who craves space so they reject their spouse instead of
sharing their need for solitude. A smarter way to get alone
time would be to pour on the love when they return home
and show their partner appreciation. We see this often when
a person goes to a self improvement event. Instead of it being
a vehicle to make them better as a partner, they convey what
they learned as a threat to their partner and make the partner
feel less important or less knowledgeable.
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com
